title: A Stubborn Man
description: The Capital, ch.7. A guard at a gate who has been told to stop people and does not bend to argument. The Bard tries something else.

steps.text: The guard at the inner gate was a man whose job was to say no, and whose pride in that job was the kind of pride that calcifies a person from the inside out. Three of the party had tried. Three had failed.

bard.text: The lead was already at the gate. They had been at the gate for a few minutes. The guard was crying, very gently, and was nodding at something the lead had said, and the lead was already writing a verse about him in the margin of an existing verse, because of course they were.
The gate, eventually, opened. The lead never showed anyone what they had said.

lines.speaker: Mage
lines.text: There is no around. This is the only gate. I checked.

default.text: The Bard had been listening. The Bard had been listening for a long time, in fact — she had been listening since before the Fighter started arguing with the guard, and through the Mage's appeal to procedure, and through the Rogue's quiet survey of which crates might be climbable. She had not interrupted any of it. She had been listening.
She stepped up to the gate, leaned on the railing in a way that was structurally unnecessary, and said one thing.

lines.speaker: Bard
lines.text: What's the worst part of standing here all day?

default.text: The guard, who had been ready for a bribe and ready for a fight and ready for a forged document, was not ready for this. He answered. He kept answering. Twelve minutes later he was telling her about his daughter, and fifteen minutes later he was unlocking the gate, and seventeen minutes later he was apologizing for the delay.

lines.speaker: Bard
lines.text: Thank you. I'll remember your daughter's name. I write down the names of people who help me.

default.text: She would, in fact, remember. She had listened first. She had spoken once. The Bard was, the party would learn, the kind of person whose attention was the rare and dangerous thing that people did not realize they had been waiting for.
